title: Early Britain: Stone Age to Romans
overview:
Britain's story begins thousands of years ago when the first people arrived during the Stone Age. These early inhabitants were hunter-gatherers who eventually learned to farm and built impressive monuments like Stonehenge in Wiltshire, which still stands today.

The Bronze Age (around 2500 BC) brought new skills in metalworking. People made bronze tools and weapons, and built round barrows (burial mounds) that can still be seen across the countryside. During the Iron Age (around 800 BC to AD 43), Celtic tribes arrived, built hill forts for protection, and introduced the first coins to Britain.

In AD 43, the Roman Emperor Claudius successfully invaded Britain, beginning nearly 400 years of Roman rule. The Romans transformed Britain by building roads, towns, public baths, and defensive walls. They brought their legal system, language (Latin), and introduced Christianity. One of the most famous Roman structures is Hadrian's Wall, built across northern England to defend against tribes from the north. The Celtic queen Boudicca led a major revolt against Roman rule in AD 60, and today a statue of her stands near Westminster Bridge in London. When the Romans left around AD 410, they left behind a network of roads, towns, and the foundations of British law that still influence the UK today.
key_facts: Stone Age people arrived in Britain thousands of years ago and were hunter-gatherers.
Stonehenge was built during the Stone Age and still stands in Wiltshire today.
Bronze Age (c. 2500 BC): People learned to make bronze tools and weapons.
Round barrows are Bronze Age burial mounds visible across the British countryside.
Iron Age (c. 800 BC to AD 43): Celtic tribes arrived and built hill forts.
The first coins in Britain were introduced during the Iron Age.
AD 43: Roman Emperor Claudius successfully invaded Britain.
Roman rule lasted nearly 400 years (AD 43 to around AD 410).
Boudicca was a Celtic queen who led a major revolt against the Romans in AD 60.
A statue of Boudicca stands on Westminster Bridge in London.
Hadrian's Wall was built by the Romans across northern England to defend against northern tribes.
Romans built roads, towns, public baths, and introduced their legal system.
Romans introduced Christianity and the Latin language to Britain.
The Romans left Britain around AD 410, but their legacy remains in roads and laws.
memory_hook: Think of ancient Britain in three metal ages: Stone (Stonehenge), Bronze (burial mounds), and Iron (hill forts) – then the Romans arrived with roads and walls that we can still walk on today.

questions.prompt: What famous Stone Age monument still stands in Wiltshire?
questions.explanation: Stonehenge is a prehistoric monument built during the Stone Age that still stands in Wiltshire today.

questions.prompt: Which Roman Emperor invaded Britain in AD 43?
questions.explanation: Emperor Claudius successfully invaded Britain in AD 43, beginning nearly 400 years of Roman rule.

questions.prompt: Who was the Celtic queen who led a revolt against the Romans?
questions.explanation: Boudicca was a Celtic queen who led a major uprising against Roman occupation in AD 60. A statue of her stands near Westminster Bridge in London.

questions.prompt: What did the Romans build across northern England to defend against tribes?
questions.explanation: Hadrian's Wall was built by the Romans across northern England as a defensive fortification against tribes from the north.

questions.prompt: What were round barrows used for in the Bronze Age?
questions.explanation: Round barrows are Bronze Age burial mounds that can still be seen across the British countryside.

questions.prompt: When did the Romans leave Britain?
questions.explanation: The Romans left Britain around AD 410, after ruling for nearly 400 years.

questions.prompt: What type of structures did Iron Age people build for protection?
questions.explanation: During the Iron Age, Celtic tribes built hill forts as defensive structures for protection.

questions.prompt: Which language did the Romans bring to Britain?
questions.explanation: The Romans brought Latin to Britain, along with their legal system and Christianity.
